title: Sine's Derivative — and the Equation That Makes Waves

md: You probably know already:
$\dfrac{d(\sin \theta)}{d\theta} = \cos \theta$

Most textbooks state this as a formula to memorize. **But where does it come from?** And — more importantly — why does this single fact explain why springs vibrate, strings ring, currents oscillate, and light propagates as a wave?

Thompson's *Calculus Made Easy* gives a geometric proof in three short steps. Then a one-paragraph observation reveals why sine/cosine are not just two functions in a long list — they are the **unique** functions that make oscillation possible.

blocks.title: Prerequisite — one trig identity
blocks.body:
From any trig textbook (sum-to-product):

$\sin M - \sin N = 2 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{M+N}{2}\right) \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{M-N}{2}\right)$

We'll use this once and then drop it. If you haven't met it before, accept it on faith — it's a consequence of the angle-addition formulas.

md: ## 1. Differentiating sine — geometrically
Let $y = \sin \theta$. We want $dy / d\theta$ — the *little bit of $y$* per *little bit of $\theta$*. As $\theta$ increases by a small angle $d\theta$, the height $y$ changes by
$dy = \sin(\theta + d\theta) - \sin\theta$
Apply the sum-to-product identity with $M = \theta + d\theta$ and $N = \theta$:
$dy = 2 \cos\!\left(\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}d\theta\right) \cdot \sin\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}d\theta\right)$
Now **two simplifications** that are valid when $d\theta$ is tiny:
**(a)** Inside the cosine, $\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}d\theta$ is essentially just $\theta$ (the $d\theta/2$ correction is vanishingly small). So $\cos(\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}d\theta) \approx \cos\theta$.

**(b)** For tiny angles, $\sin x \approx x$ (in radians). This is the **small-angle approximation** — geometrically, when the angle is small, the sine and the arc are indistinguishable. So $\sin(\tfrac{1}{2}d\theta) \approx \tfrac{1}{2}d\theta$.
Substituting both:
$dy \approx 2 \cos\theta \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}d\theta = \cos\theta \cdot d\theta$
Divide by $d\theta$:
$\boxed{\;\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} = \cos\theta\;}$

blocks.title: What just happened
blocks.body:
We used the same kind of "drop the second-order small" reasoning that powered the growing-square derivation of $d(x^2)/dx = 2x$. The pattern is: write the increment exactly, then drop everything that contains $(d\theta)^2$ or higher.

The small-angle approximation $\sin(d\theta) \approx d\theta$ is actually a statement that the difference is $O((d\theta)^3)$ — third-order small. It's not 'cheating'; it's a clean limit.

md: ## 2. Cosine falls out as a bonus
Use the identity $\cos\theta = \sin(\tfrac{\pi}{2} - \theta)$ and apply the chain rule:
$\dfrac{d(\cos\theta)}{d\theta} = \dfrac{d}{d\theta}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2} - \theta\right) = \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2} - \theta\right) \cdot (-1) = -\sin\theta$
$\boxed{\;\dfrac{d(\cos\theta)}{d\theta} = -\sin\theta\;}$
Same shape, sign flipped. **Cosine starts at 1 and decreases**; sine starts at 0 and increases. That sign difference is encoded in the derivatives.
And by the quotient rule on $\tan\theta = \sin\theta / \cos\theta$ you can show $\dfrac{d(\tan\theta)}{d\theta} = \sec^2\theta$. (Try it as an exercise.)

blocks.title: Important: all of this assumes radians
blocks.body:
The small-angle approximation $\sin x \approx x$ is only true if $x$ is measured in **radians**. If you plug degrees into these derivative formulas, you'll be wrong by a factor of $\pi / 180 \approx 0.0175$.

This is *why* calculus uses radians by default. Radians are the natural unit where derivative formulas come out clean — degrees are an arbitrary historical choice (360 ≈ days in a year, roughly).

md: ## 3. The most consequential observation in elementary calculus
Differentiate sine twice:
$\sin\theta \xrightarrow{\ d/d\theta\ } \cos\theta \xrightarrow{\ d/d\theta\ } -\sin\theta$
Differentiating twice gives back the **same function with a minus sign**. In symbols:
$\dfrac{d^2(\sin\theta)}{d\theta^2} = -\sin\theta$
Same for cosine. Together: sine and cosine are functions $y$ that satisfy
$\dfrac{d^2 y}{d\theta^2} = -y$
And — astonishingly — they are the **only** functions (up to multiplier and shift) with this property.

blocks.title: What "$y'' = -y$" describes
blocks.body:
Reading the equation aloud: *"the acceleration of $y$ is proportional to $y$ itself, but pulling the OPPOSITE way."*

This is the equation of a **restoring force**. A mass on a spring: the further you stretch it, the harder the spring pulls *back*. Push a pendulum off vertical: gravity pulls it *back*. Squeeze a wave on a string: tension snaps it *back*. 

All of these obey $y'' = -ky$. And the solutions are *always* sines and cosines (or combinations of them). That is why oscillation is sinusoidal — not because of some quirk, but because the only functions whose second derivative is proportional to *minus themselves* are sines and cosines.

md: ### See it move

The widget below animates a mass-on-spring undergoing simple harmonic motion: $y(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$. Watch the three curves track in lockstep — position (blue, solid), velocity (green, dashed), acceleration (red, dashed). Notice:
**(a) Velocity peaks when position is ZERO** (mass passing through equilibrium at full speed).
**(b) Acceleration peaks when position is at the EXTREMES $\pm A$** (maximum restoring force).
**(c) Acceleration and position are MIRROR IMAGES (negatives of each other, rescaled).** That's the visual proof of $y'' = -\omega^2 \, y$ — the SHM equation in action.
Slide the amplitude $A$ and frequency $\omega$ — see how the period, max speed, and max acceleration change.
## 4. The leap from oscillation to waves
If oscillations propagate in space — a wave on a string, an electromagnetic wave in vacuum — you upgrade to the **wave equation**:
$\dfrac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = v^2 \dfrac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}$
where $v$ is the wave speed. The simplest solution is $y(x, t) = \sin(kx - \omega t)$ — a traveling sine wave. **Same sine; same property; bigger stage.**

Try it: substitute $y = \sin(kx - \omega t)$. The left side gives $-\omega^2 \sin(kx - \omega t)$. The right side gives $-v^2 k^2 \sin(kx - \omega t)$. The equation is satisfied iff $\omega = vk$ — the **dispersion relation** that ties wavelength to frequency in every wave you've ever met.

From sound to light to ocean swells to your phone's WiFi signal — the substrate is different but the math is the same equation, with the same sine solutions, for the same reason: $\dfrac{d^2}{d\theta^2}(\sin\theta) = -\sin\theta$.
## 5. Working derivatives — a small table
$\dfrac{d}{d\theta}(\sin\theta) = \cos\theta$
$\dfrac{d}{d\theta}(\cos\theta) = -\sin\theta$
$\dfrac{d}{d\theta}(\tan\theta) = \sec^2\theta$
$\dfrac{d}{d\theta}(\sin(k\theta)) = k \cos(k\theta)$   *(chain rule)*
$\dfrac{d}{d\theta}(\sin^2\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$
These are the workhorses. You can derive the rest (sec, csc, cot, etc.) using the product/quotient/chain rules.

blocks.title: The summary
blocks.body:
Differentiating sine gives cosine — proven from the sum-to-product identity plus the small-angle approximation, no harder than algebra.

Differentiating sine *twice* gives back $-\sin\theta$. That property is unique to sines and cosines, and is the reason every oscillating thing in nature traces a sine wave: springs, pendulums, AC current, vibrating strings, light. 

When you study differential equations later, you'll see the equation $y'' + \omega^2 y = 0$ has $y = A\sin(\omega t) + B\cos(\omega t)$ as its general solution. Now you know *why*.
